title: [AggressiveInstCombine] Match the pattern and generate ctlz function call
author: rohitaggarwal007
description: Recognize the algorithm using the DeBruijnClz value to count the leading zero and replace it hardware ctlz call.

tests.test_body:
@ZSTD_countLeadingZeros32_fallback.DeBruijnClz = external hidden unnamed_addr constant [32 x i32], align 16

define dso_local i32 @ZSTD_countLeadingZeros32_fallback(i32 noundef %val) local_unnamed_addr {
entry:
  %cmp = icmp ne i32 %val, 0
  %conv = zext i1 %cmp to i32
  %call = tail call i32 (i32, ...) @assert(i32 noundef %conv)
  %shr = lshr i32 %val, 1
  %or = or i32 %shr, %val
  %shr1 = lshr i32 %or, 2
  %or2 = or i32 %shr1, %or
  %shr3 = lshr i32 %or2, 4
  %or4 = or i32 %shr3, %or2
  %shr5 = lshr i32 %or4, 8
  %or6 = or i32 %shr5, %or4
  %shr7 = lshr i32 %or6, 16
  %or8 = or i32 %shr7, %or6
  %mul = mul i32 %or8, 130329821
  %shr9 = lshr i32 %mul, 27
  %idxprom = zext i32 %shr9 to i64
  %arrayidx = getelementptr inbounds [32 x i32], ptr @ZSTD_countLeadingZeros32_fallback.DeBruijnClz, i64 0, i64 %idxprom
  %0 = load i32, ptr %arrayidx, align 4
  %sub = sub i32 31, %0
  ret i32 %sub
}

declare i32 @assert(...) local_unnamed_addr

comments.author: rohitaggarwal007
rohitaggarwal007
comments.body:
@nikic Please let me know your view.

Is any additional more details are needed for the PR?

comments.author: nikic
comments.body:
> But the files used for comparison don't have pattern as during preprocessing of source code, a builtin call was used instead.

I think this demonstrates that the fold is not useful. Yes, projects may use this pattern *if the builtin is not available*, but in our case the builtin is available, and should be used instead of the fallback implementation.

If this fallback code path is never actually used in practice, why are we implementing pattern matching for it?

comments.author: rohitaggarwal007
comments.body:
> > But the files used for comparison don't have pattern as during preprocessing of source code, a builtin call was used instead.
> 
> I think this demonstrates that the fold is not useful. Yes, projects may use this pattern _if the builtin is not available_, but in our case the builtin is available, and should be used instead of the fallback implementation.
> 
> If this fallback code path is never actually used in practice, why are we implementing pattern matching for it?

This fallback pattern is used in upcoming 2026 SPEC. SPEC is using the fallback instead of builtin. So optimizing the pattern will improve the SPEC.

comments.author: venkataramanankumar
nikic
comments.body: Wow, that's a staggering display of incompetence by SPEC, but I guess it's sufficient motivation to do this.

comments.author: davemgreen
comments.body:
From my experience there are many people who can't or wont use compiler builtins if they are not part of a standard or can be considered part of the tested subset of the compiler. Especially for FuSa but also portability reasons between different compilers. The compiler should still be able to optimize them successfully and do the best it can - especially if GCC already does the same. Although this one is likely just for SPEC.

Anyway - When we added DeBruijn for ctz (or whichever it was), part of that was to make sure that if the midend converted tablelookup -> ctz, then that didn't make any backends worse and if they preferred the table lookup then they could convert ctz->tablelookup. Will this conversion already be profitable for the backends without a clz (or reverse and ctz)?

comments.author: topperc
comments.body:
> Wow, that's a staggering display of incompetence by SPEC, but I guess it's sufficient motivation to do this.

I don't have any specific knowledge about the SPEC decision, but maybe they wanted to put all possible compilers on an even playing field? If one compiler has a builtin and another doesn't, the compiler with the builtin got a free optimization hint.
